This is another good example of what I don't enjoy about punk. They mostly seem pretty competent, apart from the singer; I can't tell if the off-key vocals is an 'artistic choice' or if he just can't sing. But then, production-wise, the bass tone sounds like it's been routed through some literal mud and the drummer sounds like he's being recorded on a mic on the other side of the room. Hard pass.
There's a nugget of something here; but then his own head distracted him and he just seemed to give up on it!
This has strong kids show vibes, but it's jaunty and I can't deny it.

Just think of the time dedicated to this being released. They had to record it (fine, maybe they did it live in a room in one take, rather than recording each part individually), then someone had to mix it, then it got mastered. Apparently it got released as a single too, which means cover art, printing, distribution logistics. And they released a video. For this. Mind blowing.
This certainly sounds more competently produced than the other punk entry on the playlist.

Apologies for a bit of a downer of an entry. Forgot to submit in a timely manner and panicked. On reflection, being angry at previously living in a small village in a post-industrial region and the political/cultural fallout that grew from closure of several industries doesn't reflect the entire nation. 
 I'm glad the rest of the playlist is a bit cheerier.
I grant you. there IS something quite British about a crowd of people shouting 'Də də-də də-dəə!' along to this.
I don't know that I have the same affinity for Mr Bowie as you do (or as Powerman 5000 do, for that matter), but I can get behind the intent to celebrate something.

I mean ... the title and the chorus have 'England' in it.
I guess you can consider it more representative of the everyday concerns of the populace than what we currently have ...

I'll be honest, it's still rather low-energy!
BUT, it's one of the few entries that is at least somewhat celebratory, rather than deeply cynical, so you gotta get something for that. I also like that it's grounded as an ode to _things_ that she loves that the country has produced; and not a whiff of a god or a monarch!

I can't rationalise really any part of this as British! And you've got far more confidence in the rhythmic capability of the British people than I do, if you expect them to attempt to sing along to this as a crowd.

I can see where you're coming from with this, but it does feel a bit bleak!
I do quite like the imagery of defiance of the past in "Kicking stones at yesterday's ghosts", though.
